text: Trochars or ports are placed to access the abdominal cavity. Small hernias are visible in the abdominal wall. In the left portion of the screen, the inflamed and dilated appendix is observed among the small bowel, with dilated blood vessels on its surface and a white exudate or inflammatory rind surrounding it.
The video shows a normal appendix, which appears soft and pliable without any signs of dilation, engorged blood vessels, or inflammatory rind.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to gently separate the inflammatory rind from the appendix and adjacent structures during the dissection. The appendix is grasped, and some bleeding from the dilated blood vessels is visible, which is controlled using an electrosurgical instrument.

The surgeon uses an instrument to divide attachments, exposing the base of the appendix. Suction is applied along the mesentery surrounding the appendix, where the appendiceal artery is located. Despite inflammation, the artery is localized and divided, with any bleeding controlled using clips, resulting in minimal blood loss.

The surgeon divides the appendiceal artery and proceeds with sharp dissection using electrocautery to control bleeding. Blunt dissection is used to gently separate tissues from the appendix, ensuring complete exposure of the cecum to prevent leaving any residual appendix tissue.

The surgeon skeletonizes the base of the inflamed appendix, removing additional tissue to secure the staple line. The final mesenteric attachments are divided, and a stapling device is placed at the base of the appendix, removing a small portion of the cecum to ensure complete removal of the appendix.

The surgeon places the removed appendix into a bag to isolate it from the abdominal cavity. The staple line is inspected for bleeding, and the region is irrigated to remove any remaining infectious fluids. The uterus is lifted to access the deep pelvis, where irrigation is performed to prevent pus accumulation and potential post-operative pelvic abscess.
The surgeon removes the appendix from the abdominal cavity using an endocatch bag, minimizing the risk of wound infection.
